content.text: Multimodal machine learning requires a decision that shapes the entire architecture: at which point in the pipeline should information from different modalities be combined? Two answers define opposite ends of the design space.
merges raw or lightly processed features from each modality before passing them through a shared deep encoder.
runs each modality through its own independent encoder to a compact embedding, then combines those embeddings at the very end. Every multimodal model sits somewhere on this spectrum.

The distinction is not just organisational — it determines what kinds of interactions are possible, which tasks succeed, whether missing modalities break inference, and how the model generalises to new domains. Understanding the trade-off is prerequisite to selecting or designing a fusion architecture.

attrs.alt: Early vs late fusion diagram: early fusion concatenates tokens before a shared transformer; late fusion runs independent encoders and merges final embeddings; comparison table of key dimensions.

content.text: In early fusion, the representation from each modality is projected into a shared embedding space and the resulting tokens are concatenated or interleaved into a single sequence before any deep processing. A single transformer (or other shared backbone) then attends across all tokens jointly — visual patches and text tokens alike appear as positions in the same attention matrix.

This is the dominant architecture for vision-language models (VLMs) such as LLaVA, BEiT-3, UnifiedIO, and Flamingo. An image is encoded into patch embeddings (e.g., 256 or 576 tokens), optionally projected through a connector MLP, and then prepended to the token sequence. The LLM processes the combined sequence with standard self-attention — visual patches and text are first-class citizens at every layer.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from transformers import CLIPVisionModel, AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from PIL import Image
import requests

class EarlyFusionVLM(nn.Module):
    """
    Minimal early-fusion VLM: image patch tokens prepended to text tokens
    before entering a shared decoder-only LLM.
    Matches the LLaVA-style architecture (LLaVA-1.5, PaliGemma, etc.).
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        vision_model_id: str = "openai/clip-vit-base-patch32",
        llm_id: str = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B",
        vision_dim: int = 768,
        llm_dim: int = 2048,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        # Frozen vision encoder: image → patch embeddings
        self.vision_encoder = CLIPVisionModel.from_pretrained(vision_model_id)
        for p in self.vision_encoder.parameters():
            p.requires_grad = False

        # Trainable connector: project vision dim → LLM embedding dim
        self.connector = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(vision_dim, llm_dim),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(llm_dim, llm_dim),
        )

        # LLM (shared backbone that processes all tokens)
        self.llm = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(llm_id)
        self.embed = self.llm.get_input_embeddings()

    def encode_image(self, pixel_values: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Returns patch embeddings projected into LLM space."""
        with torch.no_grad():
            vis_out = self.vision_encoder(pixel_values=pixel_values, output_hidden_states=True)
        # Use penultimate hidden state, drop [CLS] token
        patch_feats = vis_out.last_hidden_state[:, 1:]   # (B, N_patches, vision_dim)
        return self.connector(patch_feats)               # (B, N_patches, llm_dim)

    def forward(
        self,
        pixel_values: torch.Tensor,   # (B, 3, H, W)
        input_ids: torch.Tensor,      # (B, L)  — text tokens
        labels: torch.Tensor = None,  # (B, N_patches+L) for training
    ):
        # 1. Encode image → projected patch tokens
        vis_embeds = self.encode_image(pixel_values)       # (B, N, d_llm)
        # 2. Embed text tokens
        txt_embeds = self.embed(input_ids)                  # (B, L, d_llm)
        # 3. Early fusion: concat [image tokens || text tokens]
        inputs_embeds = torch.cat([vis_embeds, txt_embeds], dim=1)
        # 4. Shared LLM processes joint sequence — cross-modal attention at every layer
        return self.llm(inputs_embeds=inputs_embeds, labels=labels)

The shared attention mechanism gives every text token full visibility into every patch token at every layer. When the model is asked "What colour is the car on the left?", the attention heads at layer 3 can directly compare the word "left" with spatial patch positions; layer 8 might extract colour from patches; layer 20 might reason over the composed representation. This layered cross-modal interaction is unavailable to late fusion, where modalities never interact until a final merge.
The cost is the input budget. A 336 px image with 14 px patches produces 576 patch tokens. At LLaVA-NeXT's maximum tiling (5 tiles), a single image occupies
tokens, consuming most of an 8 K context window before any text appears. Processing ten images in a single context becomes impractical without compression (Q-Former, Perceiver resampler).
In late fusion each modality is processed by its own dedicated encoder to produce a fixed-size embedding vector — a

token, mean-pooled representation, or projected output. These embeddings are combined only at the final stage, typically by concatenation, element-wise addition, or a learned attention over modality vectors. The two encoders share no weights and never exchange information while encoding.

CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) is the canonical late-fusion model. A ViT image encoder and a transformer text encoder each produce a 512-dimensional embedding. Training maximises the cosine similarity between matched image–text pairs and minimises it for mismatched pairs (InfoNCE contrastive loss). At inference, image and text embeddings can be compared by dot product — enabling zero-shot classification, cross-modal retrieval, and semantic search at scale.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import CLIPVisionModel, CLIPTextModel, CLIPProcessor

class DualEncoderLateFusion(nn.Module):
    """
    Late-fusion dual encoder: independent image and text towers,
    aligned by contrastive training (CLIP-style).
    At inference: embed once → retrieve by dot product.
    """
    def __init__(self, model_id: str = "openai/clip-vit-base-patch32", embed_dim: int = 512):
        super().__init__()
        self.vision_encoder = CLIPVisionModel.from_pretrained(model_id)
        self.text_encoder   = CLIPTextModel.from_pretrained(model_id)
        # Projection heads to shared embedding space
        v_hidden = self.vision_encoder.config.hidden_size   # e.g. 768
        t_hidden = self.text_encoder.config.hidden_size     # e.g. 512
        self.vision_proj = nn.Linear(v_hidden, embed_dim, bias=False)
        self.text_proj   = nn.Linear(t_hidden, embed_dim, bias=False)
        self.logit_scale = nn.Parameter(torch.ones([]) * torch.log(torch.tensor(1/0.07)))

    def encode_image(self, pixel_values: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Image → L2-normalised embedding. Can be precomputed offline."""
        vis = self.vision_encoder(pixel_values=pixel_values).pooler_output  # (B, v_hidden)
        return F.normalize(self.vision_proj(vis), dim=-1)                   # (B, embed_dim)

    def encode_text(self, input_ids: torch.Tensor, attention_mask: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Text → L2-normalised embedding. Can be precomputed offline."""
        txt = self.text_encoder(input_ids=input_ids,
                                attention_mask=attention_mask).pooler_output  # (B, t_hidden)
        return F.normalize(self.text_proj(txt), dim=-1)                       # (B, embed_dim)

    def forward(self, pixel_values, input_ids, attention_mask):
        img_emb = self.encode_image(pixel_values)         # (B, d)
        txt_emb = self.encode_text(input_ids, attention_mask)  # (B, d)
        # Late fusion: the ONLY modality interaction is this dot product
        scale   = self.logit_scale.exp()                  # learnable temperature
        logits  = scale * img_emb @ txt_emb.T             # (B, B) similarity matrix
        # InfoNCE loss: diagonal = matched pairs
        labels  = torch.arange(len(img_emb), device=img_emb.device)
        loss_i  = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels)         # image→text
        loss_t  = F.cross_entropy(logits.T, labels)       # text→image
        return (loss_i + loss_t) / 2

    @torch.no_grad()
    def zero_shot_classify(
        self,
        image_embeddings: torch.Tensor,  # (N_imgs, d) — precomputed
        class_descriptions: list[str],   # text prompt per class
        processor,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Assign each image to its closest class by embedding similarity."""
        inputs = processor(text=class_descriptions, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
        class_embs = self.encode_text(**inputs)            # (C, d)
        # cosine similarity: (N_imgs, C)
        sims = image_embeddings @ class_embs.T
        return sims.argmax(dim=-1)

from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel
from PIL import Image
import torch, requests

model     = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
model.eval()

# --- Precompute a gallery of image embeddings (offline, once) ---
gallery_images = [Image.open(p) for p in ["cat.jpg", "dog.jpg", "car.jpg"]]
gallery_inputs = processor(images=gallery_images, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    gallery_embs = model.get_image_features(**gallery_inputs)
    gallery_embs = gallery_embs / gallery_embs.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

# --- Query by text at runtime (no images required) ---
query = "a fluffy animal sitting on a sofa"
text_inputs = processor(text=[query], return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
with torch.no_grad():
    query_emb = model.get_text_features(**text_inputs)
    query_emb = query_emb / query_emb.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

sims   = (query_emb @ gallery_embs.T).squeeze()   # (3,)
idx    = sims.argmax().item()
labels = ["cat", "dog", "car"]
print(f"Best match: {labels[idx]} (similarity={sims[idx]:.3f})")
# Asymmetric retrieval: one text query against thousands of image embeddings
# is just a batched dot product — O(N) lookups with precomputed gallery.

Between pure early and pure late fusion sits a spectrum of hybrid designs. These are important in practice because they often outperform either extreme on tasks that require partial cross-modal reasoning but also benefit from modality-specific representations.

: two separate transformer stacks (one per modality) exchange information through cross-attention at selected intermediate layers. Each stream maintains modality-specific representations while selectively attending to the other. This preserves the efficiency of late fusion for shallow layers while enabling deep interaction.

: Feature-wise Linear Modulation applies a text-conditioned affine transformation γ·x + β to intermediate feature maps in the vision tower. The text encoder produces (γ, β) scalars; the vision encoder's activations are modulated without any additional attention. Effective for visual question answering where the question specifies which visual features to amplify.

: the text stream is processed by the LLM's self-attention; visual features are brought in via added cross-attention layers between every transformer block. The two streams share no self-attention weights — closer to late fusion — but visual features are available at every LLM layer.

: a small fixed-size set of "fusion tokens" attends to both modality streams. Only the fusion tokens need to be expensive; modality-specific tokens remain cheap. Used in Perceiver Resampler (Flamingo) and BLIP-2's Q-Former.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class FiLMLayer(nn.Module):
    """
    Feature-wise Linear Modulation: text-conditioned affine transform on vision features.
    γ and β are predicted from the text representation — no cross-attention needed.
    """
    def __init__(self, vision_dim: int = 512, text_dim: int = 768):
        super().__init__()
        # Predict affine parameters from text embedding
        self.gamma_net = nn.Linear(text_dim, vision_dim)  # scale
        self.beta_net  = nn.Linear(text_dim, vision_dim)  # shift

    def forward(
        self,
        vision_features: torch.Tensor,  # (B, N, vision_dim) — patch tokens
        text_embedding: torch.Tensor,   # (B, text_dim)      — [CLS] from text encoder
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        gamma = self.gamma_net(text_embedding).unsqueeze(1)  # (B, 1, vision_dim)
        beta  = self.beta_net(text_embedding).unsqueeze(1)   # (B, 1, vision_dim)
        # Affine modulation: each patch token scaled/shifted by question-specific params
        return gamma * vision_features + beta                 # (B, N, vision_dim)


class VQAWithFiLM(nn.Module):
    """
    VQA model: text encoder conditions vision features via FiLM,
    then the modulated vision representation answers the question.
    Intermediate fusion: cheaper than full early fusion, richer than late fusion.
    """
    def __init__(self, num_classes: int = 3129):  # VQAv2 answer vocabulary
        super().__init__()
        from transformers import ViTModel, BertModel
        self.vision_enc = ViTModel.from_pretrained("google/vit-base-patch16-224")
        self.text_enc   = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
        self.film       = FiLMLayer(vision_dim=768, text_dim=768)
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(768, 1024), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.1),
            nn.Linear(1024, num_classes)
        )

    def forward(self, pixel_values, input_ids, attention_mask):
        # 1. Encode modalities independently (late-fusion style up to this point)
        vis = self.vision_enc(pixel_values=pixel_values).last_hidden_state  # (B, 197, 768)
        txt_cls = self.text_enc(input_ids=input_ids,
                                attention_mask=attention_mask).pooler_output  # (B, 768)
        # 2. Intermediate fusion: text modulates vision features
        vis_conditioned = self.film(vis[:, 1:], txt_cls)  # drop [CLS], condition patches
        # 3. Pool modulated features and classify
        pooled = vis_conditioned.mean(dim=1)               # (B, 768)
        return self.classifier(pooled)                     # (B, num_classes)
Late-fusion models are typically trained with InfoNCE contrastive loss over a batch of
image–text pairs. The objective forces matched pairs to have high cosine similarity and unmatched pairs to have low similarity. With batch size N, there are N positive pairs and N² − N negatives.

is the dot product of L2-normalised embeddings and τ (tau) is a learnable temperature. CLIP trains this loss symmetrically (also i→j direction). The effectiveness scales dramatically with batch size: CLIP was trained with effective batch sizes of 32 768, giving 32 768² ≈ 10⁹ implicit negatives per step.

def info_nce_loss(image_embeddings: torch.Tensor, text_embeddings: torch.Tensor,
                   temperature: float = 0.07) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Symmetric InfoNCE loss used in CLIP.
    image_embeddings, text_embeddings: (N, d), L2-normalised.
    temperature: scalar (or learnable logit_scale.exp() in CLIP).
    """
    N = image_embeddings.size(0)
    # (N, N) cosine similarity matrix
    logits = (image_embeddings @ text_embeddings.T) / temperature
    # Diagonal = matched pairs (positives)
    labels = torch.arange(N, device=logits.device)
    # Symmetric: image→text and text→image
    loss_i2t = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits,   labels)
    loss_t2i = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits.T, labels)
    return (loss_i2t + loss_t2i) / 2

# At scale (CLIP), batch size is 32 768:
# each forward pass sees ~10^9 implicit negatives through the N×N matrix.
# Most compute is in the two independent towers, not the O(N^2) loss.
Neither strategy is universally superior. The right choice depends on the task, the available training data, and the inference constraints.
Pre-compute gallery embeddings; query by dot product
Each encoder runs independently; missing modality = zero embedding
Early fusion over 100 frames is intractable
Spatial patch positions must interact with referring expressions
Text class descriptions embedded at inference; no retraining needed
Fine-grained alignment + modality-specific pre-training each required
Synchrony = high similarity; no reasoning required beyond alignment

: if one modality dominates the token count (576 image tokens vs 30 text tokens), attention is often absorbed by the majority modality. Use connector compression (Q-Former, resampler) or positional weighting to rebalance.

: within a large batch, some "negative" pairs may actually be semantically matched (two images of cats paired with each other's captions). At batch size 32K this becomes common. CLIP partially mitigates this via data curation; SigLIP uses sigmoid loss which naturally handles this without the normalization denominator.

: projecting a rich 196-token image representation down to a single 512-dim vector discards spatial structure. Late-fusion models therefore struggle with tasks requiring localisation ("what is in the top-left corner?") without adding region features or spatial attention heads.
Anchor the projection head during fine-tuning

: when fine-tuning a CLIP-style model on a downstream task, the projection heads trained under contrastive loss should usually be discarded and replaced with a task-specific head. Keeping contrastive heads and fine-tuning can collapse the shared embedding space.

: CLIP's zero-shot performance varies significantly with the text template. "A photo of a {class}" consistently outperforms bare class names by several percentage points on ImageNet. Use ensembled prompts (average of 80 curated templates) for best results.

# Ensembled prompts for CLIP zero-shot — outperforms single prompt
import torch, torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor

model     = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32").eval()
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

classnames = ["cat", "dog", "car"]

# Multiple prompt templates — ensemble by averaging embeddings
templates = [
    "a photo of a {}.",
    "a photograph of a {}.",
    "a rendering of a {}.",
    "an image of a {}.",
]

def zeroshot_classifier(classnames, templates):
    with torch.no_grad():
        class_embeddings = []
        for classname in classnames:
            texts = [t.format(classname) for t in templates]
            inputs = processor(text=texts, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
            embs = model.get_text_features(**inputs)
            embs = embs / embs.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
            class_embeddings.append(embs.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True))  # average templates
        return F.normalize(torch.cat(class_embeddings, dim=0), dim=-1)  # (C, d)

class_embs = zeroshot_classifier(classnames, templates)  # (3, 512)
print(f"Zero-shot classifier built for {len(classnames)} classes.")

concatenates modality tokens before a shared encoder, enabling full cross-modal attention at every layer. It is the standard architecture for VLMs (LLaVA, PaliGemma, BEiT-3) and excels at fine-grained reasoning tasks where spatial and semantic features must interact deeply.

encodes each modality independently and merges fixed-size embeddings at the output. It is the architecture of CLIP, ImageBind, and most cross-modal retrieval systems. Its key strengths are efficiency (precomputed galleries), missing-modality robustness, and zero-shot transfer via text prompting.

(co-attention, FiLM, cross-attention injection) occupies the middle ground and is often the right choice when you need modality-specific pre-training and some degree of cross-modal reasoning, but cannot afford full joint attention over all tokens.

The InfoNCE contrastive objective trained at massive scale (400M+ pairs, batch size 32K+) drives late-fusion alignment. Its effectiveness scales with batch size because the N×N similarity matrix provides N² − N implicit negatives per step.
Practical choice heuristic: if your task needs
in the image, use early fusion. If it needs
(retrieval), use late fusion. If both, use a hybrid.
